summary: Strong second set with Let It Grow, Morning Dew; mixed first half
blurb: Second‑set soundboard capture is crystal clear, delivering standout Let It Grow, Morning Dew and Eyes Of The World. First set suffers from a noisy matrix audience feed and uneven jams, with Deal notably weak. Overall the show swings between high‑energy peaks and flat stretches.
review: The Oct 25, 1985 Sportatorium gig is a study in contrasts. While the first set is hampered by an over‑bright, click‑riddled matrix recording that makes Jerry’s vocals sound “more to offer vocally than Weir” and leaves Deal sounding limp, the second half shines thanks to a superb SBD source – listeners hear a “clear, balanced, super sounding” mix that lets the band stretch out. Highlights include a soaring Let It Grow, a haunting Morning Dew that kicks off set two, and an inspired Eyes Of The World segue from Estimated Prophet. Phil’s bass drops “tons of bombs,” Brent holds down solid keyboards, and the dual drummers toggle between explosive energy and occasional chaos. Fans who can tolerate the rough first‑set tape will be rewarded by one of the stronger mid‑80s Florida performances.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Mixed sentiments across recordings with two clearly positive reviews and three mixed; consistent praise for second‑set material (Let It Grow, Morning Dew, Eyes Of The World) outweighs the first‑set complaints, yielding a modest positive adjustment.

best_recording.reason: Second‑set soundboard source is clear, balanced and captures the band at its best; first set remains noisy but overall this recording offers the most complete and highest‑quality listening experience.

key_highlights: Second‑set SBD capture delivers crystal‑clear sound and strong performances of Let It Grow, Morning Dew and Eyes Of The World.
Jerry’s vocals receive special praise on the first set despite overall mixed playing; Phil’s bass bombs are a noted low‑end highlight.
Brent provides solid, well‑balanced keyboard work even as other elements fluctuate.
Recording quality varies widely – noisy matrix audience tape for Set 1 versus superb SBD for Set 2.

band_performance.Jerry: Vocals are highlighted as a strong point (“more to offer vocally than Weir”), though some reviewers find his overall playing flat and lackluster; guitar tone is noted as solid in parts.
band_performance.Phil: Delivers “tons of Phil bombs” with a pronounced low‑end bass presence that anchors the band, especially on the audience tape.
band_performance.Bob: Receives criticism for sounding off‑key and possibly intoxicated; no positive remarks are recorded.
band_performance.Brent: Provides solid, well‑balanced keyboard work, keeping the harmonic foundation steady amid mixed overall performance assessments.
band_performance.Mickey: Drumming is described as both explosive and chaotic, reflecting an uneven but energetic contribution.
band_performance.Billy: Shares the dual‑drum duties with Mickey; his playing swings between powerful bursts and moments of stepping on each other.
